narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown solid determination with your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the typical level for students in our dataset, and your middle-family-income background that aligns with many others. Your school decile rankings of 7th in both Year 1 and Year 3 place you comfortably above average, reflecting stronger-than-typical academic performance early and later in your studies. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority—over 92%—of students who go this route. However, the model's prediction for you came in at a 47% probability of failing the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold that led to your application being rejected, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 24, which is notably lower than the average of about 37 and had the strongest influence in tipping the scales against you. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile ranking was the most powerful counterforce, strongly pulling the prediction toward passing by showing your later academic growth. Your Year 1 decile also helped in that direction, though with less impact, while your GPA provided a modest boost toward success. Even your family income and full-time status nudged things slightly toward passing, but they played smaller roles overall. Compared to typical students, your grades stand out positively, yet the LSAT gap was the dominant concern that outweighed these strengths for this model's decision. We understand this is disappointing, especially given the unique challenges many Black women face in competitive fields like law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor—reach out to prep resources tailored for underrepresented students, and reapply once you've strengthened that area. You're clearly capable based on your grades, and with targeted effort, law school doors can open wider for you. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
